stories.headline: DUAL BLOCKADE: US AND IRAN SIMULTANEOUSLY CLOSING HORMUZ — BLOOMBERG WARNS SHIPPING 'MONTHS FROM NORMAL'
stories.body: Today's Bloomberg report confirms the crisis has evolved into an unprecedented 'dual blockade' — the US naval blockade of Iranian ports (active since April 13) and Iran's retaliatory closure of the Strait to all foreign shipping operating simultaneously for the first time in history. Three dozen oil executives, traders, and shippers tell Bloomberg the world 'hasn't grasped the severity,' with shipowners warning that a return to normal routing is months away at best. WTI closed Friday at $96.07, but Brent — carrying the full Hormuz exposure — is set to re-price materially higher at Monday's open.

stories.headline: GAS PRICES UP $1.16/GALLON SINCE WAR START; $5 NATIONAL AVERAGE THRESHOLD IN VIEW AS BRENT EYES $105+ MONDAY
stories.body: U.S. average gas prices have risen $1.16/gallon since the conflict began, with analysts warning the $5.00 national average is in sight if Hormuz stays closed. Brent settled at $105.07 Thursday before Friday's WTI close at $96.07 — the $9/bbl spread between the two benchmarks reflects Brent's direct Hormuz exposure. Energy CPI is roughly 7% of the headline basket, meaning a further $10 Brent move adds approximately 0.4 ppt to headline CPI — a direct input into Thursday's Core PCE print and the Fed's policy calculus.

stories.headline: IMF STAGFLATION CALL FIRMS: 3.1% GLOBAL GROWTH AND 4.4% INFLATION AS HORMUZ CRISIS ENTERS WEEK THREE
stories.body: The IMF's April 2026 WEO cut global growth to 3.1% and raised inflation to 4.4%, with the adverse scenario projecting 2.5% growth and inflation above 5.4% under sustained Hormuz closure. Today's dual-blockade confirmation shifts the probability distribution toward that adverse case. The IMF labels this the largest oil-market disruption in history — roughly 20% of global crude flow affected. The 10-year Treasury at 4.38% (+7 bps Friday) reflects expanding breakevens and term premium widening against a growth slowdown backdrop — the classic stagflation fingerprint in the rates market.

stories.headline: FED IN TEXTBOOK SUPPLY-SHOCK BIND AHEAD OF CORE PCE THURSDAY AND MAY 5-6 FOMC
stories.body: The FOMC holds at 3.50–3.75% with one cut still penciled in the 2026 dot plot, but fed funds futures have already pushed the first cut to September or later. The dual blockade sharpens the dilemma: rate hikes fight the inflation leg but deepen the growth wound, while cuts risk entrenching supply-driven price gains into broader expectations. Core PCE on Thursday (April 30) is the pivotal print — if core services ex-housing is rising due to energy cost passthrough via logistics and manufacturing, the Fed's September cut becomes harder to defend. May 5-6 statement language on 'transitory' vs. 'structural' is more informative than any rate action.

stories.headline: GOLD CONSOLIDATES NEAR $4,689 AFTER $4,738 RECORD; EQUITIES FACE DUAL-BLOCKADE REPRICING AT MONDAY'S OPEN
stories.body: Gold's -1.0% Friday pullback looks like tactical profit-taking after printing a new all-time high of $4,738 earlier in the week — the year-to-date stagflation trade remains the dominant macro expression of 2026. The S&P 500 (-0.37%) and Nasdaq (-0.51%) closed lower Friday. With Bloomberg confirming today that the Hormuz shutdown is open-ended, Monday's equity open will face a fresh round of supply-shock risk premium repricing. The DXY bounced to 98.82 (+0.3%) on safe-haven demand, though sustained dollar strength is complicated by the same growth drag that is pressuring risk assets globally.

stories.headline: MICHIGAN'S CHAMPIONSHIP RESHAPES RECRUITING CYCLE AS DECLARATION DEADLINE AND PORTAL CLOSE LOCK 2026-27 ROSTERS
stories.body: Michigan's 69-63 national title win over UConn on April 6 — the Wolverines' first championship since 1989 — has driven significant roster movement across college basketball. The April 24 underclassman declaration deadline and April 21 portal close are both now passed, locking rosters for 2026-27. Key portal final commitments: 7-foot-2 C Moustapha Thiam to Michigan, John Blackwell (Wisconsin) to Duke, and Terrence Brown (Utah, 19.9 PPG) to UNC. Washington added LeJuan Watts (Texas Tech, HM All-WCC at WSU) on April 25 as its most recent portal addition, bringing the Huskies to 10 roster players with five spots remaining.

stories.headline: EDWARDS (BONE BRUISE, MULTIPLE WEEKS) AND DIVINCENZO (ACHILLES, SEASON OVER) BOTH HURT AS T-WOLVES WIN GAME 4 — DOSUNMU DROPS 43
stories.body: Minnesota took a 3-1 series lead over Denver in Game 4 despite losing Anthony Edwards (left knee bone bruise/hyperextension, multiple weeks, no ligament damage) and Donte DiVincenzo (Achilles tear, season over) in the same game. Ayo Dosunmu filled the void with 43 points. Edwards is the T-Wolves' franchise player and co-MVP candidate — his absence dramatically reshapes any potential second-round path. In other first-round action today: the Lakers (up 3-0 vs. Rockets) look to sweep at 9:30 PM ET, Celtics host the Sixers in Game 4 at 7 PM ET, and Cleveland plays Toronto.

stories.headline: 2026 NFL DRAFT COMPLETE: SEAHAWKS TURN 2 PICKS INTO 8-PIECE CLASS; NUSSMEIER FALLS TO CHIEFS AT #249; MURDOCK MR. IRRELEVANT
stories.body: The 2026 NFL Draft concluded Saturday with 257 selections. Seattle's Day 3 haul via trades: G Beau Stephens (#148, Iowa), WR Emmanuel Henderson Jr. (#199, Kansas), CB Andre Fuller (#236, Toledo), NT Deven Eastern (#242, Minnesota), CB Michael Dansby (#255, Arizona) — converting 2 Day 3 picks into 5. Full Seahawks class: 8 picks total across all 3 days. LSU QB Garrett Nussmeier fell all the way to the Chiefs at #249; Tennessee CB Jerrod McCoy (knee concerns) slid to the Raiders as the first Round 4 pick. Denver LB Red Murdock (2nd nationally in tackles in 2024-25) was Mr. Irrelevant at #257.

stories.headline: NOTRE DAME BLUE-GOLD SPRING GAME ENDS 41-40 ON PORATH WALK-OFF 43-YARDER AS SPRING SEASON WRAPS NATIONWIDE
stories.body: Notre Dame's 2026 Blue-Gold Game ended Saturday in dramatic fashion — the offense edged the defense 41-40 on a Spencer Porath walk-off 43-yard field goal, a close result signaling an improved Irish defense heading into fall. Spring game season is effectively complete across the Power Four, with Indiana, Michigan, and several Big Ten programs also wrapping this week. Washington's spring concluded April 2 (game held at Seahawks VMAC). Attention now shifts to summer camps, August transfer portal window strategy, and fall camp depth-chart battles — the last real roster adjustment point before the season.

sports.detail: No game today. Trail Orlando Magic 1-2 in East first-round series. Game 4 Monday April 27 in Orlando at 8 PM ET — near must-win with Cunningham needing a bounce-back after being limited in Game 3.
Completed 3-game sweep of Cardinals at Busch Stadium (3-2 Fri, 11-9 Sat, W today for 3rd straight). Naylor's clutch HR sparked the series opener. Season improves to ~14-15. Next series TBD.

8-pick class complete: Price (RB, #32), Clark (S, #64), Neal (CB, #99), Stephens (G, #148), Henderson Jr. (WR, #199), Fuller (CB, #236), Eastern (NT, #242), Dansby (CB, #255). Day 3 trades converted 2 picks into 5.

Spring complete (game April 2 at Seahawks VMAC, HC Fisch also serving as OC). Top 2027 targets visiting: 4-star DL Tevita Nonu (O'Dea HS, top in-state), OL Benjamin Lowther. Season opener Sept 5 vs. Washington State (Big Ten).

Added LeJuan Watts (Texas Tech, April 25) — 33 games/28 starts for TTU, HM All-WCC at WSU. Roster at 10 (5 spots open). Key losses: Diallo + Claude (29 PPG combined), Kepnang, Steinbach (NBA draft), Muldrew, Mandaquit.

watchlist: Core PCE Thursday 4/30 8:30 AM ET — core services ex-housing is the oil pass-through signal the Fed is watching
May 5-6 FOMC — no cut expected; 'transitory' vs. 'structural' framing on Hormuz shock is the key signal
Monday oil market open — Brent likely to gap higher on dual-blockade confirmation from Bloomberg today
Pistons vs. Magic Game 4 Monday in Orlando, 8 PM ET — series 1-2 Detroit, near elimination pressure
T-Wolves vs. Nuggets Game 5 — Dosunmu must carry load with Edwards (out multiple weeks) and DiVincenzo (Achilles, season over) both sidelined

deepDive.title: The Stagflation Policy Bind: Why Supply Shocks Break the Fed's Standard Playbook
content: Today's Bloomberg headline — 'Iran War: Hormuz Double Blockade Halts Ship Traffic, Dims Hope for the Economy' — captures a scenario that has haunted central bankers since the 1970s: a supply shock that simultaneously worsens inflation and kills growth. The IMF cut 2026 global growth to 3.1% while lifting inflation to 4.4%. That combination is uniquely difficult for policymakers because the tools that fight inflation (rate hikes) directly amplify the growth slowdown, while the tools that fight recession (rate cuts) risk entrenching supply-driven price gains into broader expectations — a policy trap with no clean exit.

The Fed's standard framework, the Taylor Rule, responds to two gaps: inflation above target and output below potential. In a demand shock, both gaps move together — an overheating economy calls for hikes; a recession calls for cuts. Supply shocks break this symmetry by pushing the gaps in opposite directions. When oil spikes from a physical disruption, headline inflation rises immediately through direct passthrough (energy is roughly 7% of the CPI basket), core inflation follows via transportation and manufacturing costs, while real GDP falls as higher energy costs compress consumer spending and business margins. No single rate action closes both gaps simultaneously — the Fed is forced to choose which one to target.

The 1973 Arab oil embargo is the canonical reference. OPEC's production cut quadrupled Brent in weeks; the Nixon-era Fed, already behind the inflation curve, failed to tighten aggressively and allowed expectations to become unanchored. CPI hit 12.3% in 1974 alongside GDP contraction of -0.5%. The lesson most draw from Volcker's subsequent success is 'always fight inflation first.' But Volcker's 1979-82 shock worked in part because the supply disruption had already resolved — the core problem was entrenched expectations, not ongoing physical scarcity. Today differs: Bloomberg's consensus from three dozen oil executives and traders is that normal Hormuz shipping is 'months away at best,' making this an open-ended disruption rather than a dated shock with a visible expiration.

Core PCE on Thursday (April 30) is the pivotal print. If core services ex-housing is rising — picking up energy cost passthrough via logistics, airfares, and manufacturing — the Fed will read it as evidence the shock is embedding into expectations, making a September cut much harder to defend. The dot plot still pencils one 2026 cut, but fed funds futures have already pushed it to September or later. At the May 5-6 FOMC, the statement language on 'transitory' versus 'structural' is more informative than any rate action. Watch for any softening of the March 'prepared to adjust' phrasing — its omission would signal the Fed has chosen to prioritize inflation over growth even as the IMF's 3.1% global growth baseline continues to deteriorate.
